planting.note: Divide clumps carefully in early spring or sow fresh seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade in spring; Yunnan meadowrue resents disturbance and establishes slowly — plant in its permanent position, mulch well, and leave undivided for the best long-term performance.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Yunnan meadowrue (Delavay's meadow-rue) is a Chinese shade perennial that produces one of the most refined and beautiful flower clouds of any tall shade perennial — the slender, wiry stems rise 4–6 feet above large, delicate, glaucous, blue-green, columbine-like compound leaves, then branch into enormous, open, airy panicles of hundreds of small, pendant, lilac-purple to lavender flowers with large, showy, creamy-yellow stamens in mid-summer. The effect is supremely light and airy — a transparent wash of purple above the foliage — and the long, wiry flower stalks move gracefully in the slightest breeze. 'Hewitt's Double' (sterile, fully double, deep-mauve pompon flowers with no stamens) is the most widely grown cultivar and has the most ornamental and longest-lasting flowers. Slightly more tolerant of warmer climates (to zone 9) than T. rochebrunianum. An exceptional specimen for the back of large shaded borders and cottage-style shade gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds on the soil surface (do not cover — seeds require light for germination) in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in fall for spring germination, or in very early spring; Welsh poppy self-seeds so freely in suitable shaded, moist conditions that a single planting typically results in permanent, self-sustaining colonies — subsequent hand-sowing is rarely necessary.

directions: Surface-sow seeds on moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in fall or early spring; Welsh poppy is a European woodland and rocky-ledge annual or short-lived perennial — unique among poppies as the only species that genuinely thrives in shade — producing a continuous succession of cheerful, bright-orange or yellow, four-petaled, simple poppy flowers from late spring through summer above rosettes of divided, bright-green, fernlike leaves. It is also unique among its family in tolerating and actually preferring cool, shaded, moist woodland conditions, making it an invaluable source of cheerful color in the shade garden at a season when few other shade plants offer comparable brightness. It self-seeds prolifically in suitable conditions to form permanent, self-renewing colonies under trees and in shaded rocky crevices. The double-flowered form, with fully petaled orange or yellow flowers, is particularly ornamental. Unlike the Himalayan blue meconopsis, Welsh poppy is genuinely easy to grow and is essentially maintenance-free in a suitable site. Considered invasive in some Pacific Northwest gardens where conditions perfectly suit it.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant tubers 2–3 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in fall before ground freezes; spring fumewort is a spring ephemeral — it emerges and blooms early, sets seed, and then dies down completely by early summer, leaving bare soil, so mark its position and interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in fall; spring fumewort (fumitory, bird-in-a-bush) is a European woodland spring ephemeral bulb in the fumitory family — one of the earliest woodland bulbs to flower, emerging and blooming in early spring (March–April) simultaneously with snowdrops, early crocuses, and Scilla sibirica, before the deciduous tree canopy leafs out and shades the ground. The compact plants produce racemes of small, tube-spurred, two-lipped flowers in shades of deep rose-pink, magenta, red, lilac, or white with darker purple tips, above finely divided, gray-green, fernlike leaves. Like other spring ephemerals, it completes its entire growth cycle — emergence, flowering, seed-set, and dormancy — before deciduous trees fully shade the ground. It naturalizes readily under deciduous trees, spreads by self-seeding and by the division of the solid round tubers. The named cultivar 'George Baker' (deep, vivid salmon-red) is considered the finest individual form. Plant in large drifts of 25 or more bulbs for best effect under deciduous shade.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Rodgersia podophylla has the most striking bronze spring foliage coloring of all the rodgersias and is best sited where the morning sun or a dappled canopy reveals this emerging bronze coloring — deep full shade suppresses the bronze tones.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; bronze shield rodgersia is a Japanese and Korean shade perennial with the most architecturally dramatic foliage of all the rodgersias — the large, hand-shaped, palmate leaves with five to nine toothed, glossy leaflets open in spring in a vivid bronze to copper-brown coloring, gradually transitioning through the season to bright green, then returning to bronze, copper, and red tints in autumn. The bronze coloring intensifies in spring and fall when the plant receives some sunlight; deep shade suppresses the coloring but the large, architectural foliage remains handsome throughout. Tall, creamy-white, astilbe-like flower plumes are produced in midsummer well above the foliage. Rodgersia podophylla forms slowly spreading colonies by thick, creeping rhizomes and is best suited to permanently moist, partly shaded positions beside water, in wet woodland gardens, or in rain gardens where the rhizomes can spread unimpeded. The large-scale combination of bold architectural foliage and tall summer flowers makes it one of the most impressive large shade garden perennials available.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in early spring; Arisaema ringens is more tolerant of mild winter temperatures than many Asian arisaema species and is one of the better choices for zone 7–9 woodland gardens, where other species may not reliably overwinter.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; ringed cobra lily is a Japanese and Chinese woodland Arisaema species with one of the most dramatic and architecturally distinctive flower structures of the genus — the broad, rounded, glossy spathe (hooded 'pulpit') is boldly striped with deep purple-brown, greenish, and white bands running vertically, and the hood is dramatically arched forward and downward with a prominent, beak-like tip, giving the entire flower the striking appearance of a rearing cobra with a patterned hood — the basis for the species' common name. Two large, three-parted leaves, each with three broad leaflets, emerge from a single tuber. Like all Arisaema species, the plants are deciduous and go completely dormant by midsummer. Ringed cobra lily is among the most cold-tolerant of the Asian arisaemas and performs better in zone 7–9 gardens than many other species. It requires cool, moist, acidic, humus-rich woodland soil and resents summer heat and drought. Over time a single tuber produces offsets to form small colonies. A distinctive collector's plant for the serious woodland garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
